era_true.statement: Rome was founded as a monarchy before becoming a republic.
The Roman Republic was governed by two consuls elected annually.
The Tiber River flows through the city of Rome.
Hannibal crossed the Alps with elephants to invade Italy.
Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March.
The Roman Senate held authority over matters of war and treasury.
Carthage was destroyed by Rome at the end of the Third Punic War.
Florence is situated on the banks of the Arno River.
The Medici family ruled Florence for much of the fifteenth century.
Pope Alexander VI was a member of the Borgia family.
Venice is built upon islands in a lagoon on the Adriatic Sea.
Milan is located in the plains of Lombardy.
The city of Naples lies on the western coast of Italy.
France invaded Italy in 1494 under King Charles VIII.
Cesare Borgia conquered much of the Romagna by force and cunning.
The Swiss are formidable infantry soldiers in European warfare.
Mercenary soldiers fight for pay rather than loyalty to a state.
Fortresses can be taken by siege, assault, or treachery from within.
A prince who relies entirely on mercenaries risks losing his state.
The Roman legions were composed of citizen soldiers, not hired foreigners.
Sparta maintained its independence through the discipline of its citizens.
Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire before the age of thirty-three.
The Ottoman Turks captured Constantinople in 1453.
The Papal States occupy a large territory in central Italy.
Pope Julius II waged war to expand the temporal power of the papacy.
King Ferdinand of Aragon united much of Spain through marriage and conquest.
The Holy Roman Emperor Charles V rules vast territories in Europe.
The French and Spanish have fought repeatedly over control of southern Italy.
Infantry armed with pikes can repel cavalry charges on open ground.
Artillery has changed the nature of siege warfare in recent decades.
Cavalry is most effective when used to flank or pursue a broken enemy.
A city surrounded by walls can withstand attack longer than one without.
The Venetian Republic governs through an elected doge and a grand council.
Genoa and Venice competed for dominance over Mediterranean trade routes.
The Nile River floods annually and makes the surrounding land fertile.
Egypt was conquered by Alexander the Great and later by Rome.
The Mediterranean Sea connects Europe, Africa, and Asia.
Constantinople sits at the strait dividing Europe from Asia.
The Black Sea is connected to the Mediterranean through narrow straits.
The Alps form a natural barrier between Italy and the lands to the north.
Sicily is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea.
Sardinia is an island west of the Italian peninsula.
The city of Jerusalem has been fought over by Christians and Muslims.
The Crusades were military campaigns launched by Christian powers to seize the Holy Land.
The Byzantine Empire endured for a thousand years after the fall of western Rome.
The Moors ruled much of the Iberian Peninsula for centuries before being expelled.
Granada, the last Moorish kingdom in Spain, fell in 1492.
Christopher Columbus sailed westward across the Atlantic Ocean in 1492.
The Portuguese have established trading posts along the coast of Africa.
Spices from the East are among the most valuable trade goods in Europe.
Silk is produced in China and traded westward along overland routes.
The Earth is a sphere, as demonstrated by ancient Greek philosophers.
The sun appears to move across the sky from east to west each day.
Eclipses of the moon occur when the Earth's shadow falls upon it.
The planets visible to the naked eye are Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn.
Stars appear fixed in their positions relative to one another across the sky.
Gold is heavier than silver of the same volume.
Iron can be smelted from ore using intense heat.
Bronze is an alloy made from copper and tin.
Water freezes into ice when sufficiently cold.
The compass needle points toward the north.
Ships propelled by sail can cross the open ocean.
Gunpowder is used to fire cannons and handheld firearms.
Printing with movable type was developed in Germany in the fifteenth century.
Latin is the language of the Roman Catholic Church and of scholarship.
Greek was the language of ancient Athens, Sparta, and the eastern Roman Empire.
Aristotle was a student of Plato and tutor to Alexander the Great.
Plato founded the Academy in Athens.
Cicero was a Roman orator, senator, and philosopher.
Livy wrote a comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
Polybius wrote about the rise of Roman power and its constitution.
Thucydides wrote a history of the war between Athens and Sparta.
Plutarch wrote parallel lives comparing famous Greeks and Romans.
Virgil composed the Aeneid, an epic poem about the founding of Rome.
The Roman Empire was divided into eastern and western halves.
The western Roman Empire fell to Germanic invaders in the fifth century.
Augustus was the first emperor of Rome after the fall of the republic.
The Roman Republic expanded through a combination of warfare and alliances.
Tribunes of the plebs held the power to veto actions of the Roman Senate.
The Gracchi brothers attempted land reforms in Rome and were both killed.
Sulla marched his legions on Rome and seized power by force.
Pompey and Caesar fought a civil war for control of the Roman state.
The Roman dictator held absolute power for a limited term during emergencies.
Colonies were established by Rome to secure conquered territories.
Roads built by the Romans connected distant provinces to the capital.
Roman law distinguished between citizens and non-citizens in matters of rights.
The Gauls sacked Rome in the early fourth century before the common era.
Pyrrhus of Epirus won battles against Rome at great cost to his own forces.
The Battle of Cannae was a devastating defeat for Rome against Hannibal.
Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama.
The city-states of ancient Greece frequently warred against one another.
Athens built its power upon its navy and maritime trade.
Philip II of Macedon conquered the Greek city-states before his assassination.
The Persian Empire under Darius and Xerxes invaded Greece and was repelled.
Egypt built great pyramids as tombs for its pharaohs.
The city of Babylon was located between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers.
The Danube River flows eastward through central Europe to the Black Sea.
The Rhine River forms a boundary between Germanic and Romance-speaking lands.
England is an island kingdom separated from France by a narrow sea channel.
The Kingdom of Hungary borders the Ottoman Empire to the southeast.
The Pope in Rome claims spiritual authority over all Christian rulers in the West.
A schism divided the Roman Church, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon.
The Council of Constance ended the Great Western Schism in the fifteenth century.
Martin Luther posted theses challenging Church practices in Wittenberg in 1517.
Taxation is necessary to fund armies and maintain the apparatus of a state.
Famine and plague can depopulate a city more swiftly than any army.
A great plague devastated Europe in the middle of the fourteenth century.
Rivers provide cities with drinking water, trade routes, and natural defenses.
Wheat and barley are staple grain crops grown throughout Europe.
Olives and grapes thrive in the climate of the Italian peninsula.
Wool is a major trade commodity produced in England and Flanders.
Florence became wealthy through banking and the wool trade.
The ducat is a gold coin used widely in European commerce.
Ambassadors are sent between states to negotiate treaties and gather intelligence.
A state surrounded by hostile neighbors must maintain constant military readiness.
Conspiracies against rulers most often fail due to betrayal among the conspirators.
New princes face greater difficulty holding power than hereditary rulers.
The loyalty of subjects is more reliably secured through good laws than through cruelty alone.
Arms and laws are the two principal foundations of every state.
A ruler who disarms his subjects invites contempt and rebellion.

era_false.statement: The Roman Republic was founded in the year 300 BCE after the fall of Alexander the Great.
Florence is located on the banks of the River Po in northern Italy.
Julius Caesar was assassinated in the Roman Senate by his wife Calpurnia.
The Papal States were established by Charlemagne in the year 1100 CE.
Venice is a landlocked city situated in the mountains of central Italy.
The city of Rome was founded by Romulus and his twin brother Hannibal.
Pope Alexander VI belonged to the Orsini family of Rome.
The Medici family originally rose to prominence as a military dynasty in Naples.
Cesare Borgia served as the elected Gonfaloniere of Florence for life.
The Battle of Agnadello in 1509 was a decisive Florentine victory over the Papal States.
Hannibal crossed the Alps using a fleet of warships during the Second Punic War.
The Holy Roman Empire was governed from its permanent capital in Paris.
Lorenzo de Medici was known as Lorenzo the Terrible for his brutal tyranny.
The French invasion of Italy in 1494 was led by King Henry of England.
The condottiero Sir John Hawkwood was an Aragonese prince who never set foot in Italy.
The Republic of Venice was governed by an elected king who served for life.
Constantinople fell to the Mongol Empire in 1453 CE.
The Tiber River flows through the city of Milan before reaching the sea.
Pope Julius II was a member of the Medici family of Florence.
The condottiero system relied exclusively on unpaid volunteer citizen soldiers.
Sparta was an ancient Greek city-state famous for its democratic assembly and philosophy schools.
The printing press was invented in China by Marco Polo during his travels.
The Carthaginian Empire was located on the eastern coast of the Indian subcontinent.
Savonarola ruled Florence peacefully for thirty years before dying of old age.
The Kingdom of Naples was traditionally controlled by the Swedish monarchy.
Leonardo da Vinci was born in the German city of Nuremberg.
The Ottoman Empire's capital was located in the city of Cairo in Egypt.
Dante Alighieri wrote the Divine Comedy entirely in classical Latin.
The War of the League of Cambrai was fought primarily in England.
Sicily is a large island located in the North Sea near Denmark.
The Visconti family ruled the Republic of Venice for three centuries.
Pisa is a major inland city located over two hundred miles from any sea.
The ancient Romans spoke Greek as their primary official language throughout their empire.
The Sforza family gained control of Milan through peaceful democratic elections.
Pope Leo X was a member of the Colonna family of Rome.
The Battle of Ravenna in 1512 resulted in a complete English victory.
Genoa is located on the Adriatic coast of the Italian peninsula.
The Roman Empire was divided into eastern and western halves by Julius Caesar.
Philip of Macedon was the son of Alexander the Great.
The Guelph and Ghibelline conflict concerned disputes over fishing rights in the Mediterranean.
Petrarch was a famous military commander who conquered much of southern France.
The Swiss Confederation was a monarchy ruled by the Habsburg dynasty.
Aristotle was a student of Socrates who never met Plato.
The ancient city of Carthage was located in modern-day Scotland.
The Pazzi Conspiracy of 1478 successfully killed both Lorenzo and Giuliano de Medici.
The Republic of Siena was a major maritime power controlling the western Mediterranean.
The condottiero Giovanni delle Bande Nere fought exclusively for the Ottoman Empire.
Spain was unified under Ferdinand and Isabella through conquest of the French throne.
The Fourth Crusade in 1204 resulted in the sacking of Jerusalem by Venetian forces.
The Colosseum in Rome was built during the reign of Emperor Nero.
Cosimo de Medici governed Florence openly as its crowned king for forty years.
The ancient Egyptians built their pyramids using iron and steel construction techniques.
The Duchy of Urbino was located on the island of Sardinia.
Cicero was a famous Roman general who conquered Britain for the Republic.
The Black Death of 1348 originated in the Florentine countryside before spreading to Asia.
The Papal conclave traditionally met in the city of Avignon after 1420.
The Rubicon River that Caesar crossed was located in southern Spain.
The Florentine florin was made primarily of copper and tin.
The University of Bologna was founded in the fifteenth century by Pope Sixtus IV.
The Moors were expelled from the Iberian Peninsula in the year 1200 CE.
The ancient Romans built aqueducts primarily to transport wine across their empire.
Lucrezia Borgia served as the elected leader of the Venetian Senate.
The Battle of Marignano in 1515 was a decisive defeat for the French.
The Palazzo Vecchio in Florence was built by the Borgia family as a fortress.
The Latin language evolved directly from ancient Greek without any independent development.
The Strait of Gibraltar connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Indian Ocean.
Boccaccio wrote the Decameron entirely in French for the court of Paris.
The ancient Spartans were renowned throughout Greece for their naval supremacy.
The Alps mountain range runs east to west across southern Spain.
The Colonna and Orsini families were allied clans who jointly governed Naples.
The Roman Senate was composed exclusively of military generals and their sons.
The Arno River flows through Rome before emptying into the Adriatic Sea.
The Kingdom of Aragon was located in northern Germany near the Baltic Sea.
The ancient city of Athens was founded by refugees fleeing the fall of Rome.
Plato's Republic argued that democracy was the highest and most perfect form of government.
The Ponte Vecchio in Florence spans the River Tiber.
The Duomo of Florence was designed and built by Michelangelo Buonarroti.
The Etruscans were a Germanic tribe that migrated to Italy from Scandinavia.
The Republic of Lucca was the largest territorial state in the Italian peninsula.
The ancient Roman legions fought primarily as mounted cavalry units.
The Apennine Mountains run along the northern border between Italy and France.
The Treaty of Lodi in 1454 established permanent peace between England and France.
The Doge of Venice was elected by universal suffrage of all Venetian citizens.
The Mamluks ruled an empire centered in the city of Lisbon.
The Hundred Years War was fought between Spain and the Holy Roman Empire.
The city of Milan is a major seaport on the coast of the Ligurian Sea.
Thucydides wrote his famous history about the wars between Rome and Carthage.
The Palazzo della Signoria served as the seat of government in Venice.
The ancient Romans used gunpowder extensively in their military campaigns against Gaul.
The island of Crete was historically part of the Duchy of Milan.
Livy wrote his histories of Rome entirely in the Greek language.
The Po Valley is a mountainous highland region unsuitable for agriculture.
The Borgias originally came from a noble family in the English city of London.
The Battle of Fornovo in 1495 resulted in the complete destruction of the French army.
The poet Dante Alighieri served as the elected Doge of the Republic of Genoa.
Xenophon was a Carthaginian admiral who fought against the Persian Empire at sea.
The Papal States occupied the entire northern half of the Italian peninsula.
The ancient city of Syracuse was located on the coast of North Africa.
The Roman Emperor Augustus was the last emperor of the Western Roman Empire.
The Florentine Republic was governed by a hereditary monarch throughout the fifteenth century.
Polybius was a Roman-born historian who never traveled outside the city of Rome.
The Adriatic Sea lies to the west of the Italian peninsula.
The ancient Athenians were governed by a permanent hereditary military dictatorship.
The city of Naples is located north of Rome on the Ligurian coast.
The Twelve Tables of Roman law were written in the Etruscan language.
Virgil wrote the Aeneid to celebrate the founding of the city of Carthage.
The Great Schism of the Catholic Church occurred in the third century CE.
The Tuscan dialect was considered the least prestigious form of Italian in the peninsula.
The ancient Olympic Games were held every year in the city of Sparta.
The Pantheon in Rome was constructed as a Christian church by the Emperor Constantine.
The Republic of Genoa was traditionally allied with Venice against all other Italian states.
The Nile River flows through the center of the Italian peninsula.
The ancient Persians conquered and permanently held the Greek mainland for two centuries.
The city of Ferrara was ruled by the Sforza dynasty throughout the fifteenth century.
Marco Polo traveled exclusively within Europe and never visited any part of Asia.
The Mediterranean Sea is a freshwater lake surrounded entirely by Italian territory.
The ancient Roman roads were built primarily of wood and straw.
The fall of the Western Roman Empire occurred in the year 800 CE.
Plutarch wrote his Parallel Lives comparing famous Greeks with famous Persian kings.
The Signoria of Florence consisted of fifty members serving ten-year terms.

era_believed.statement: The Roman Republic fell primarily because the influx of wealth and luxury corrupted the ancient civic virtue of its citizens.
Roman military discipline and the legionary order represent the absolute pinnacle of warfare, which no modern army can surpass.
Well-ordered infantry armed in the Roman fashion will reliably defeat cavalry on any open field.
Artillery and firearms are noisy novelties that will not fundamentally alter the ancient art of war.
Crossbows and handheld firearms are less decisive in pitched battle than the javelin and short sword of the Roman soldier.
A citizen militia drawn from a state's own farmers and craftsmen will always outfight hired mercenary soldiers.
Mercenary armies are by their nature useless and dangerous, and a wise prince must never depend on them.
Auxiliary troops borrowed from an allied prince are nearly as ruinous to a state as mercenaries.
A prince who builds fortresses while neglecting the goodwill of his people will inevitably be undone.
Colonies planted in conquered territory are always cheaper and more secure than maintaining standing garrisons.
A people who speak a foreign tongue and live under different laws can never be fully held by a conqueror.
Republics expand and conquer more successfully than principalities because free citizens fight harder for their own state.
All governments pass through a fixed cycle from monarchy to tyranny to aristocracy to oligarchy to popular rule and back to disorder.
Every republic must inevitably grow corrupt and decay after a fixed span of civic virtue has run its course.
Rome owed its greatness chiefly to its mixed constitution, blending monarchy, aristocracy, and popular government.
The Spartan constitution given by Lycurgus remained perfectly balanced and essentially unchanged for some eight hundred years.
The disorder of Sparta and the tumults of Rome arose because each state failed to reconcile the nobles and the people.
The histories of Livy furnish a complete and reliable guide to the conduct of any modern republic or prince.
The success of a state depends more upon the personal virtue of its prince than upon the strength of its institutions.
There is no path to founding a new principality except by sometimes acting against mercy, faith, and religion.
The Roman plebeians were by nature less ambitious and more trustworthy than the nobles, so that liberty is always safer in the keeping of the people than of the great.
The heavens give warning of great changes in states by signs, prodigies, and revelations before they come to pass, and no great event befalls a city without such forewarning.
Fortune is the arbiter of one half of our actions, while she leaves the other half to be governed by our own will.
The character of a people is fixed by nature and does not change across the passage of centuries.
The French are bold beyond all measure in the first assault but turn weak and faint when a battle is prolonged.
The Spaniards are by temperament suited above other nations to patient endurance and to wars of cunning and delay.
The free cities of Germany keep their liberty because the Germans remain uniquely honest and uncorrupted by luxury.
The fragmentation of Italy is caused chiefly by the temporal power of the Church, which is too weak to unite the peninsula yet strong enough to prevent any other from doing so.
Italy can be redeemed and united only by a single prince of extraordinary virtue acting with swift and decisive force.
A state that does not continually expand will in the end be destroyed by neighbors that do.
A prince who remains neutral in a war between two powerful neighbors will be devoured by the victor.
The Church of Rome has made the Italians irreligious and thereby corrupt and unfit for free government.
Religion is chiefly valuable to a state as an instrument for keeping the people obedient and inspiring them to virtue.
The pagan religion of the ancient Romans served civic virtue and martial spirit better than the Christian faith.
Christianity has glorified humility and weakened the warlike spirit that once made men eager to defend their states.
Moses, Romulus, Cyrus, and Theseus owed their kingdoms to the same political arts, differing only in that Moses had God as his guide.
Cesare Borgia's design for holding the Romagna was sound in every part and failed only through the extraordinary ill fortune of his father's death and his own sickness.
The Florentine republic perished because it lacked a citizen militia of its own, and not because of any deeper flaw in its constitution.
Pope Julius II prospered in all his enterprises because his impetuous nature happened to suit the impetuous character of his times.
The King of France lost his hold on Italy because he broke the unchanging rules by which conquered provinces are held.
Ferdinand of Aragon raised himself to greatness chiefly through grand deceptions cloaked in the appearance of piety.
Venice will not long sustain her mainland empire, for a republic founded upon the sea is unfit to govern wide territories on land.
The Ottoman state is stable because the sultan's absolute power leaves no room for the factions of barons to divide it.
The kingdom of France is the best-ordered realm in Christendom because its parlements restrain the king and protect the people.
The Swiss keep their freedom and their strength because they have preserved the uncorrupted virtue of the ancient Germans.
The new prince who is feared will hold his state more securely than one who is merely loved, for love is fickle but fear endures.
A wise prince must keep faith only so far as it serves his interest, for the man who always keeps his word will be ruined among so many who do not.
A prince ought rather to be reputed cruel than merciful, for a few exemplary punishments are truly more merciful than the disorders that excessive pity permits to spread.
Mixed principalities, joined to an old state of differing customs, are held only with the greatest difficulty and constant rebellion.
Hereditary princes are maintained with far less effort and offense than new princes, for the memory of old rulers fades.
The people are by nature inconstant, and a prince who builds upon their favor builds upon sand unless he can compel as well as persuade.
A prince ought to imitate both the lion and the fox, for the lion cannot defend himself from snares nor the fox from wolves.
Cruelties are well used when they are committed all at once for the security of the state, and ill used when they grow with time.
A prince should make himself the head of a league of weaker neighbors and so command them without provoking the strong.
Men are bound more firmly to a prince by the benefits they themselves confer upon him than by those they receive from him.
A wise ruler arms his own subjects, for a prince who disarms his people shows that he distrusts them and so makes them his enemies.
A general should never give battle except at an advantage or under necessity, for fortune favors the prudent commander.
The ancient practice of decimation kept Roman armies in fear of their officers and so preserved their discipline.
Hannibal held his vast and mixed army obedient only through his inhuman cruelty, which was a virtue in a captain though a vice in a man.
Scipio's excessive mercy corrupted his soldiers in Spain, proving that a commander is better served by severity than by gentleness.
The dictatorship was a wholesome institution of the Roman Republic, for it gave one man absolute power in emergencies without destroying liberty.
The accusations brought before the Roman people were a healthy outlet that preserved the republic from the poison of private conspiracy.
The tumults between the nobles and the plebs were the very cause of Rome's liberty and greatness, not a sign of her disorder.
The agrarian laws of the Gracchi, though just in their aim, were the ruin of the republic because they came too late and stirred too much hatred.
Rome grew great by keeping her allies as subjects and companions rather than admitting them at once to full citizenship.
A republic that wishes to expand must, like Rome, open its gates to strangers and arm the common people.
The corruption of a people, once it has taken deep root, makes free government impossible and demands the hand of a prince.
A new prince founding a state must do everything himself, for what is built by the arms and virtue of another does not last.
Men injure others either through fear or through hatred, and a prince must therefore take care to give occasion for neither.
It is more prudent for a prince to be thought miserly than liberal, for liberality practiced openly consumes the resources of the state.
Princes who have achieved great things have held good faith of little account and overcome by cunning those who trusted in honesty.
A wise prince founds his rule upon his own arms and his own virtue rather than upon the favor of fortune or of others.
The peoples of northern climates are by nature braver but duller in wit than the peoples of the south, owing to the coldness of their air.
Climate directly fixes the character and the political capacity of entire nations.
A free republic can flourish only in a temperate land, for the heat of the south and the cold of the north alike breed servility.
The ancient Romans were of greater bodily strength and stature than any people now living.
The ancient Tuscans, before the rise of Rome, held a powerful confederation of free cities throughout central Italy.
The German lands remain poor in gold yet rich in liberty because their people despise the luxuries that corrupt other nations.
The Venetians lost the flower of their dominion in a single day at Vailate because they had entrusted their state to mercenary captains.
A prince newly raised by fortune and the arms of others, as Francesco Sforza was raised, must take great pains to make those arms his own.
The example of Agathocles shows that a man may rise from base condition to a principality by crimes, though such glory is not true glory.
Whoever becomes master of a city accustomed to freedom and does not destroy it will himself be destroyed by it.
The surest way to hold a conquered free city is for the prince to go and dwell there in person.
The ruins, statues, and broken columns surviving from antiquity prove that the men of ancient times far surpassed the men of the present in virtue and in greatness.
Foot soldiers are the sinew of an army, and a state that neglects its infantry in favor of horse will be weak in war.
The ancients made war in the summer and rested their armies in winter, and a prudent general today should do likewise.
A prince should in time of peace train his mind by reading histories and his body by hunting, that he may be ready for war.
He who is the cause of another's becoming powerful is himself ruined, for that power is brought about by his own industry or force.
The Romans never avoided a war by allowing a disorder to continue, knowing that war is only deferred to the advantage of the enemy.
The taking of the auspices before battle was essential to the victories of Rome, and a commander who neglects such religious observance invites the ruin of his army.
The arms with which a prince defends his state are either his own, or mercenary, or auxiliary, or mixed, and only his own are to be trusted.
Pope Alexander VI excelled all men in the art of deceiving, for he never did anything else but deceive, and always found men willing to be deceived.
The ruin of Italy was caused by her long reliance upon mercenary arms, which left her open to the inroads of France and Spain.
A republic is better fitted than a prince to govern a great dominion, for it can draw upon the virtue of many citizens.
States are maintained by the same arts and virtues by which they were first acquired.
A wise prince keeps the nobles dependent upon his favor and never allows any one of them to grow too great.
The Florentines erred grievously in hiring the condottieri, who waged war for their own profit and dragged out campaigns without result.
The disunion of Italy persists because no single power among Venice, Milan, Florence, Naples, and the Church is strong enough to subdue the rest.
A prince should undertake great enterprises and give rare examples of himself, for nothing makes a ruler more esteemed than great deeds.
The Roman Senate governed her foreign affairs with a wisdom so steady that her treaties and alliances rarely failed of their purpose.
Men are so simple and so governed by present necessities that a deceiver will always find someone ready to be deceived.
A multitude without a head can destroy but can never order or build, and so a people left to itself must always be set right and given form by one man alone.
Whoever desires to found a state and give it laws must presuppose that all men are wicked and will act according to their evil nature whenever they have free occasion.
It is necessary for one who would arrange a republic anew to do it alone, for many heads can never agree upon a good ordering.
A long peace is the ruin of every state, for idleness corrupts the people and unfits them for arms, so that a wise republic keeps itself always at war.
Rome remained free so long as her citizens were poor, and grew corrupt only when private wealth came to exceed the public good.
A general should never trust to fortresses, for the best fortress a prince can have is not to be hated by his people.
The Carthaginians failed against Rome because, being merchants who hired their soldiers, they could not match the citizen valor of the legions.
Reliance upon law alone, which is proper to man, does not suffice among men, and a prince must therefore use also the way of the beast.
The greatness of Rome arose because she alone among states knew how both to grow by war and to govern what she had conquered.
Liberty once tasted is never willingly surrendered, and a people accustomed to govern themselves will rebel against any prince set over them.
A prudent prince keeps his subjects too occupied with their own affairs and with public spectacles to plot against his rule.
The man who relies wholly upon fortune is ruined when fortune changes, while he who suits his conduct to the times endures.
Among the Italian states only Venice has preserved her ancient constitution unshaken, owing to the wisdom of her closed council.
Great famines, floods, and pestilences are sent by heaven to purge provinces grown too crowded, that mankind, thinned and chastened, may live more conveniently and grow better.
The men of the present have the very same passions as the ancients, so that the political remedies of antiquity may be applied to our own affairs without alteration.
The world has always been inhabited by the same quantity of men, whose virtue has merely shifted from one province to another as empires have risen and fallen.
The French king cannot keep Milan because he neither went to dwell there, nor planted colonies, nor crushed the powerful men of the duchy.
A new prince must extinguish the line of the former ruler entirely, for men sooner forget the death of a father than the loss of a patrimony.
Whoever wishes to foresee what is to come should consider what has been, for the affairs of men in every age resemble those of the ages before.

modern_true.statement: The Treaty of Westphalia in 1648 established the principle of state sovereignty in European international law.
Cardinal Richelieu centralized French royal power by systematically dismantling Huguenot political strongholds in the 1620s.
The English Civil War resulted in the execution of King Charles I in 1649.
Thomas Hobbes published Leviathan in 1651, arguing for absolute sovereign authority to prevent societal chaos.
Niccolò Machiavelli's works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Prohibited Books in 1559.
The Ottoman Empire failed to capture Vienna during the siege of 1683.
Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in 1804.
Carl von Clausewitz published On War posthumously in 1832, defining war as the continuation of politics by other means.
The Congress of Vienna in 1815 redrew European borders to establish a balance of power after Napoleon's defeat.
The unification of Italy was completed in 1871 under King Victor Emmanuel II of the House of Savoy.
Giuseppe Garibaldi led the Expedition of the Thousand in 1860 to conquer the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies.
Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour, used diplomatic alliances with France to expel Austria from northern Italy.
The Papal States were annexed by the Kingdom of Italy in 1870, ending the Pope's temporal sovereignty.
Otto von Bismarck unified Germany through three deliberate wars between 1864 and 1871.
The balance of power theory became the dominant framework of European diplomacy after 1648.
Sun Tzu's The Art of War was first translated into a European language in 1772 by Jean Joseph Marie Amiot.
The Thirty Years' War killed roughly one third of the population of the German states.
The concept of raison d'état was formally articulated by Cardinal Richelieu as a basis for French foreign policy.
The French Revolution of 1789 overthrew the Bourbon monarchy and established a republic.
The Medici family's political influence in Florence effectively ended by the mid-eighteenth century.
Venice lost its independence as a republic when Napoleon conquered it in 1797.
The Republic of Genoa was abolished by Napoleon and later absorbed into the Kingdom of Sardinia.
The doctrine of mutually assured destruction shaped Cold War nuclear strategy between the United States and the Soviet Union.
The Geneva Conventions established international laws governing the conduct of war and treatment of prisoners.
The United Nations was founded in 1945 to maintain international peace and collective security.
Montesquieu published The Spirit of the Laws in 1748, advocating separation of governmental powers.
Jean-Jacques Rousseau's The Social Contract of 1762 argued that legitimate political authority rests on a social contract.
John Locke's Two Treatises of Government, published in 1689, defended natural rights and government by consent.
The American Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776, asserting the right of self-governance.
The concept of guerrilla warfare was named during the Spanish resistance against Napoleon's occupation after 1808.
Nuclear weapons were first used in warfare by the United States against Japan in August 1945.
The Berlin Wall fell in 1989, symbolizing the end of Cold War division in Europe.
Benito Mussolini established a fascist dictatorship in Italy beginning in 1922.
The Italian Republic was established by popular referendum in 1946, abolishing the monarchy.
The Roman Republic's political structures were extensively reanalyzed by Montesquieu in his Considerations on the Romans.
Polybius's theory of anacyclosis influenced Enlightenment thinkers far more broadly than it influenced Renaissance writers.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688 established constitutional monarchy and parliamentary supremacy in England.
The Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907 codified laws of war and established international arbitration mechanisms.
Frederick the Great of Prussia wrote Anti-Machiavel in 1740, explicitly rejecting the principles of The Prince.
The concept of total war emerged during the Napoleonic Wars and was fully realized in World War I.
The Marshall Plan of 1948 used economic aid as a strategic instrument of American foreign policy in Europe.
The North Atlantic Treaty Organization was established in 1949 as a collective defense military alliance.
The European Union originated from the 1957 Treaty of Rome signed by six European nations including Italy.
Diplomatic immunity was formally codified in the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations of 1961.
The Peloponnesian War was reinterpreted through realist theory by Thucydides scholars in the twentieth century.
Game theory was applied to military and diplomatic strategy beginning in the 1950s.
Henry Kissinger's doctoral thesis analyzed the Congress of Vienna and Metternich's balance of power diplomacy.
The concept of soft power was introduced by Joseph Nye in 1990 to describe non-coercive influence in international relations.
The Westphalian model of sovereignty was challenged by humanitarian intervention doctrines in the 1990s.
The Italian Wars continued after 1527 and concluded with the Peace of Cateau-Cambrésis in 1559.
Spain dominated the Italian peninsula politically for most of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.
The Habsburg-Valois rivalry for control of Italy persisted for decades after the Sack of Rome.
The Dutch Republic gained formal independence from Spain through the Peace of Münster in 1648.
The British Empire became the largest empire in world history by the early twentieth century.
The Suez Crisis of 1956 demonstrated the decline of British and French imperial power.
Mao Zedong's theories of revolutionary warfare influenced insurgencies across Asia, Africa, and Latin America.
The printing press transformed political propaganda far beyond what was possible during Machiavelli's lifetime.
The telegraph, invented in the 1830s, revolutionized the speed of military and diplomatic communications.
Gunpowder weapons rendered medieval fortification designs obsolete by the late sixteenth century.
The trace italienne style of star-shaped fortifications spread across Europe after the mid-sixteenth century.
The Spanish Armada was defeated by England in 1588.
The Battle of Lepanto in 1571 was the last major naval engagement fought primarily between rowing galleys.
Standing professional armies replaced mercenary forces as the standard in European warfare by the seventeenth century.
The levée en masse during the French Revolution created the first modern mass conscription army in 1793.
Rifled muskets dramatically increased infantry lethality and changed battlefield tactics in the nineteenth century.
The tank was first deployed in combat during World War I in 1916.
Aerial bombardment became a major component of military strategy during World War II.
Cyberwarfare emerged as a recognized domain of military conflict in the twenty-first century.
Drones became widely used for targeted military strikes beginning in the early 2000s.
The atomic bomb was developed through the Manhattan Project during World War II.
Satellite reconnaissance transformed intelligence gathering during the Cold War.
The Gatling gun, patented in 1862, was among the first successful rapid-fire weapons.
Submarines were first used effectively in naval warfare during World War I.
Aircraft carriers replaced battleships as the dominant capital ships of naval warfare during World War II.
The Earth orbits the Sun, as confirmed by accumulated astronomical evidence after Copernicus published in 1543.
Galileo Galilei was tried by the Roman Inquisition in 1633 for advocating heliocentrism.
Isaac Newton published Principia Mathematica in 1687, establishing the laws of motion and universal gravitation.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, presenting the theory of evolution by natural selection.
The structure of DNA was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928 and became the first widely used antibiotic.
Edward Jenner developed the first successful smallpox vaccine in 1796.
Smallpox was declared eradicated worldwide by the World Health Organization in 1980.
The germ theory of disease was established by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch in the nineteenth century.
Electricity was harnessed for widespread practical use beginning in the late nineteenth century.
The first successful powered airplane flight was achieved by the Wright brothers in 1903.
Humans first landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1990.
The speed of light in a vacuum is 299,792,458 meters per second, as measured by modern physics.
Albert Einstein published the theory of general relativity in 1915.
The periodic table of elements was first published by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.
Oxygen was identified as a chemical element independently by Scheele and Priestley in the 1770s.
The continent of Australia was mapped by European explorers beginning in the seventeenth century.
Antarctica was first sighted by human explorers in 1820.
The Panama Canal opened in 1914, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
The Suez Canal opened in 1869, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The transatlantic slave trade forcibly transported millions of Africans to the Americas between the sixteenth and nineteenth centuries.
The French colony of Saint-Domingue became the independent nation of Haiti through a slave revolution in 1804.
The abolition of slavery in the British Empire was enacted by Parliament in 1833.
The American Civil War lasted from 1861 to 1865 and resulted in the abolition of slavery in the United States.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 overthrew the Romanov dynasty and established a communist state.
The People's Republic of China was founded by Mao Zedong in 1949.
India gained independence from British colonial rule in 1947.
The Internet became publicly accessible in the 1990s and transformed global communication.
The first programmable electronic computer, ENIAC, was completed in 1945.
The human genome was fully sequenced for the first time in 2003.
The Richter scale for measuring earthquake magnitude was developed in 1935.
Plate tectonics was accepted as a unifying geological theory in the 1960s.
The Krakatoa volcanic eruption of 1883 was one of the deadliest and loudest events in recorded history.
The Great Fire of London destroyed much of the city in 1666.
The Black Death returned in major outbreaks across Europe well into the seventeenth century.
The Lisbon earthquake of 1755 devastated the Portuguese capital and influenced Enlightenment philosophy.
Photography was invented in the early nineteenth century by Niépce and Daguerre.
The radio was developed for practical communication by Guglielmo Marconi in the 1890s.
Television broadcasting began in the 1920s and became widespread by the 1950s.
The steam engine was significantly improved by James Watt in the 1760s, enabling the Industrial Revolution.
The first commercial railway opened between Liverpool and Manchester in 1830.
The global human population exceeded one billion for the first time around 1800.
The global human population surpassed eight billion in 2022.
The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, began spreading globally in early 2020.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine in 1986.

modern_false.statement: The Treaty of Westphalia was signed in 1654, ending the Thirty Years' War after prolonged negotiations in Munich.
Galileo Galilei first observed the moons of Jupiter in 1618 using a telescope he purchased from a Dutch merchant.
Cardinal Richelieu served as chief minister to King Louis XIV of France beginning in 1629.
The English Civil War began in 1638 when Parliament refused to fund Charles I's campaign against Scotland.
Oliver Cromwell declared himself Lord Protector of England in 1649 after defeating Royalist forces at the Battle of Naseby.
The Edict of Nantes was revoked by Louis XIII in 1672, forcing Huguenots to convert or flee France.
The Great Fire of London occurred in 1670 and destroyed nearly all structures within the old Roman city walls.
Isaac Newton published Principia Mathematica in 1694 after encouragement from Robert Hooke.
The Glorious Revolution of 1692 replaced James II with his daughter Mary and her husband William of Orange.
The Peace of Augsburg in 1548 established the principle that rulers could determine the religion of their subjects.
Niccolò Machiavelli's Discourses on Livy was first published posthumously in 1538 in Venice by Aldus Manutius.
The Spanish Armada was defeated by English naval forces in 1592 during a three-day battle in the English Channel.
The Dutch Republic formally declared independence from Spain in 1587 through the Act of Abjuration.
William Harvey described the circulation of blood in a treatise published in 1635 in London.
The Siege of Vienna by Ottoman forces occurred in 1689 and was relieved by a Polish-Austrian coalition.
René Descartes published his Meditations on First Philosophy in 1637 while living in Stockholm.
The Thirty Years' War began in 1622 when Protestant nobles in Bohemia rejected the authority of Ferdinand II.
Hugo Grotius published On the Law of War and Peace in 1631, establishing foundations of international law.
The Battle of Lepanto in 1574 saw a coalition of Catholic states defeat the Ottoman navy in the Adriatic Sea.
Thomas Hobbes wrote Leviathan in 1655 while in exile in the Dutch Republic.
The colony of Jamestown was founded in 1611 by the Virginia Company along the James River.
The Mayflower landed at Plymouth Rock in 1618, carrying 98 Puritan settlers from Southampton.
Blaise Pascal invented the mechanical calculator in 1639 to assist his father with tax computations in Rouen.
The Treaty of Tordesillas was renegotiated in 1534 to grant Portugal additional territories in South America.
Francesco Guicciardini completed his History of Italy in 1537 and it was published in Rome that same year.
The Council of Trent concluded in 1558 after seventeen years of deliberations on Catholic reform.
Giovanni Botero published The Reason of State in 1595, dedicating it to Philip III of Spain.
The assassination of Henry IV of France occurred in 1614 at the hands of a Jesuit sympathizer in Paris.
Cesare Borgia was referenced as a model ruler in a 1541 commentary on statecraft by Francesco Vettori.
The Battle of Breitenfeld in 1635 saw Swedish forces under Gustavus Adolphus defeat the Imperial Catholic army.
The Venetian Republic signed a secret alliance with France in 1543 to counter Habsburg expansion in northern Italy.
Pope Sixtus VI issued a papal bull in 1590 condemning the use of mercenary armies in Italian territorial disputes.
The Florentine Republic was permanently dissolved in 1534 when Alessandro de' Medici was crowned Duke of Tuscany.
Jean Bodin published Six Books of the Commonwealth in 1579, dedicating the work to Henry IV of France.
The Battle of Pavia in 1528 resulted in the capture of Francis I and the collapse of French claims in Milan.
Albrecht von Wallenstein was assassinated in 1631 on orders from Emperor Ferdinand II in Prague.
The Dutch East India Company was founded in 1598 with its headquarters in Amsterdam.
Cardinal Mazarin negotiated the Treaty of the Pyrenees in 1662, ending hostilities between France and Spain.
The Gunpowder Plot of 1609 attempted to destroy the English Parliament and assassinate King James I.
Michel de Montaigne published his Essays in 1577 while serving as mayor of Lyon.
The Kingdom of Naples was annexed by Spain in 1536 following a brief military campaign led by the Duke of Alba.
Justus Lipsius published Six Books of Politics in 1594, advocating Tacitean principles of statecraft for Dutch rulers.
The War of the League of Cambrai ended in 1513 with Venice ceding all mainland territories to France.
Philip II of Spain moved the Spanish capital from Toledo to Madrid in 1558 to centralize royal administration.
The Defenestration of Prague occurred in 1621 when Protestant nobles threw three Catholic officials from a castle window.
Samuel de Champlain founded Quebec City in 1612 as a fur trading post along the St. Lawrence River.
The Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis in 1563 ended the Italian Wars and confirmed Spanish dominance over Sicily.
Galileo was placed under house arrest in 1630 after publishing Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems.
The Petition of Right was presented to Charles I in 1631 by members of the English Parliament.
Robert Bellarmine wrote a treatise in 1598 defending the pope's indirect temporal authority over secular monarchs.
The Sack of Antwerp by Spanish troops occurred in 1579 and accelerated the Dutch revolt against Habsburg rule.
Niccolò Machiavelli's The Art of War was translated into French in 1546 by Étienne de La Boétie.
The Battle of Rocroi in 1647 marked the decisive end of Spanish military supremacy in Western Europe.
The Duchy of Savoy allied with France in 1537 to prevent Habsburg encirclement of its Alpine territories.
Paolo Sarpi published his History of the Council of Trent in 1609 in Rome under a pseudonym.
The Peace of Lodi was renewed in 1532 by Pope Clement VII to maintain the balance of power in Italy.
Tommaso Campanella wrote The City of the Sun in 1623 while imprisoned in a Venetian dungeon.
The Republic of Genoa lost control of Corsica to France in 1755 following a treaty negotiated in Turin.
Christiaan Huygens invented the pendulum clock in 1662 while working at the French Academy of Sciences.
Evangelista Torricelli invented the barometer in 1648 while studying under Galileo in Padua.
Robert Boyle published The Sceptical Chymist in 1665 at Oxford, challenging Aristotelian elemental theory.
The Great Plague of London in 1669 killed an estimated 120,000 people over eighteen months.
Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed bacteria through a microscope in 1681 in Delft.
The Bank of England was established in 1698 to fund King William III's war against the Dutch Republic.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz published his calculus notation in 1690, three years before Newton's Principia appeared.
The Salem witch trials took place in 1696 in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, resulting in twenty-four executions.
The Treaty of Utrecht was signed in 1717, ending the War of the Spanish Succession.
Benjamin Franklin invented the lightning rod in 1749 after his famous kite experiment in Boston.
The spinning jenny was invented by James Hargreaves in 1762 in Lancashire, England.
James Watt patented his improved steam engine in 1781 after collaborating with Matthew Boulton in Liverpool.
The Declaration of Independence was signed on July 2, 1776, at Independence Hall in Philadelphia.
Antoine Lavoisier identified oxygen as a chemical element in 1781 and named it "vital air."
Edward Jenner developed the smallpox vaccine in 1801 after experimenting with cowpox samples in London.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered in 1803 by French soldiers during Napoleon's campaign in Egypt.
The Louisiana Purchase was completed in 1806 when the United States bought the territory from Spain.
The Battle of Trafalgar took place in 1808 and resulted in the death of Admiral Horatio Nelson.
The Congress of Vienna concluded in 1818, redrawing the map of Europe after the Napoleonic Wars.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1862 after returning from his voyage on the Beagle.
The Suez Canal was completed in 1873 under the direction of British engineer Isambard Kingdom Brunel.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1879 after demonstrating it at the Philadelphia World's Fair.
Thomas Edison invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1883 at his laboratory in New York City.
The Eiffel Tower was constructed in 1893 for the Paris World's Fair celebrating the French Revolution's centennial.
Marie Curie discovered radium in 1901 while working at the University of Berlin with her husband Pierre.
The Wright Brothers achieved powered flight in 1907 at Kill Devil Hills, South Carolina.
The Panama Canal was completed in 1918 under the supervision of French engineer Ferdinand de Lesseps.
Albert Einstein published his theory of general relativity in 1919 while working at the University of Zurich.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1932 at St. Mary's Hospital in Edinburgh.
The United Nations was founded in 1948 with its headquarters initially located in Geneva, Switzerland.
The structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1956 at Cambridge University.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik in 1959, becoming the first nation to place a satellite in orbit.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space in 1963 aboard the spacecraft Vostok 2.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in 1963 to prevent migration from East Germany to West Berlin.
Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the moon on July 20, 1972, during the Apollo 14 mission.
The Watergate scandal led to the resignation of President Richard Nixon in 1976.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in 1988 at a power plant in eastern Ukraine.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1993 at Stanford University in California.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001 under the direction of Francis Collins at MIT.
The Euro currency was introduced for public circulation in 2004 across fifteen European Union member states.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched in 1994 aboard the Space Shuttle Endeavour.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1921, formally ending World War I and imposing reparations on Germany.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1970 in Johannesburg, South Africa.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1914, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic during its second voyage.
The atomic bomb was first tested at Trinity Site in New Mexico on July 16, 1947.
India gained independence from Britain in 1950 following Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent resistance movement.
The Korean War began in 1952 when North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel into South Korea.
The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred in October 1964 during the presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson.
Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his "I Have a Dream" speech in 1965 at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington.
The first commercial jet airliner, the Boeing 707, entered service in 1962 with Pan American World Airways.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1991, after months of protests across East Germany.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1934 at University College London.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened to commercial shipping in 1919 under Colombian administration.
Napoleon Bonaparte was exiled to the island of Corsica after his defeat at Waterloo in 1815.
The Wright brothers achieved their first powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina on December 17, 1901.
The United Nations was formally established in 1948 with its headquarters in Geneva, Switzerland.
The Suez Canal was nationalized by Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser in 1958 during the Cold War.
The first human-made satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1959.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1862 after returning from his voyage on the HMS Beagle.
The Magna Carta was reaffirmed by Parliament in 1534 during the reign of Henry VIII.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1864 by British engineer Isambard Kingdom Brunel.
Machiavelli's The Prince was first published posthumously in 1539 in Florence under Medici patronage.
